story_shape: the tiny burner who terrifies safeties -- A&M's highest-paid receiver and the SEC's top deep threat

plan.spine: an undersized, electric deep threat became the highest-paid receiver in Texas A&M history and the centerpiece of a more pass-heavy 2026
blend: 2025: 59 rec, 917 yds, 4 TD (+81 rush); 207-yard CFP game vs. Notre Dame
3.25 yards per route, 3rd in the Power Four; 22 forced missed tackles
highest-paid WR in Texas A&M history (re-signed)
plan.payoff: 917 yards, a 207-yard Playoff game, and a record A&M receiver deal
plan.forward_stake: whether the deep threat becomes the SEC's most dangerous receiver

hook: Mario Craver is 5-9, 165 pounds, and a nightmare for safeties. He dropped 207 yards on Notre Dame in the Playoff and became the highest-paid receiver in Texas A&M history. Small, fast, and back for more.
expand: Craver's 2025 was a deep-ball highlight reel -- 59 catches for 917 yards, a 207-yard explosion against Notre Dame in the College Football Playoff, and a yards-per-route mark (3.25) that ranked third in the entire Power Four. At 5-9, 165, he wins the way small receivers have to: with separation speed and after-the-catch wiggle (22 forced missed tackles). Texas A&M made him the highest-paid receiver in program history to keep him. The forward stakes are a featured role in a passing game expected to throw more in 2026 -- the runway for a burner like this to go from very good to the SEC's most dangerous deep threat. For Craver, the size was always the doubt and the speed was always the answer; 2026 is where he settles it.
style: Craver is pure juice -- a vertical and after-the-catch weapon whose tape and our data agree. The play-by-play is loud: a 94th-percentile yards-per-catch, a 93rd-percentile explosive-catch rate, and a 95th-percentile EPA per target -- the chunk-play profile of a true field-stretcher, backed by a solid (76th-percentile) catch rate that says he's more than a track guy running fly routes. The 22 forced missed tackles are the YAC element: catch a five-yard slant, make the first man miss, and it's a 40-yard touchdown. At his size he's not a contested-catch or red-zone bully -- he's the speed that bends a defense's whole structure, the receiver a safety has to honor on every snap. Give him a step, and the game changes.
